title: Multi-Objective Spatial Planning
text: minimal end--end example showing intended pipeline. believe ’ve found bug mosap, please file issue (ideally reproducible example) : https://github.com/josesalgr/mosap/issues.
code: library(mosap)  # --- Example inputs (replace with your own) # pu_data            : data.frame(id, cost, locked_in?, locked_out?) # features_data      : data.frame(id, name) # dist_features_data : data.frame(pu, feature, amount)  # 1) Build the Data object x <- inputData(   pu = pu_data,   features = features_data,   dist_features = dist_features_data )  # 2) Add actions/effects/targets/spatial relations as needed x <- x |>   add_actions(actions_df) |>   add_effects(effects_df) |>   add_conservation_targets_relative(0.17) |>   add_spatial_boundary()  # 3) Register atomic objectives (aliases are later used by MO methods) x <- x |>   add_objective_min_cost(alias = "cost") |>   add_objective_min_fragmentation(alias = "frag")  # 4) Configure a multi-objective method (weighted sum) mo <- set_method_weighted(   x,   aliases = c("cost", "frag"),   weights = c(1, 1),   normalize = TRUE )  # 5) Configure solver (example; adapt to your solver and preferences) mo <- set_solver(   mo,   solver = "auto",   time_limit = 60,   gap_limit = 0.01,   verbose = TRUE )  # 6) Solve res <- solve(mo)  # 7) Inspect results (examples) get_pu(res, only_selected = TRUE) get_actions(res, only_selected = TRUE) get_features(res) get_targets(res)

title: Add management actions to a planning problem — add_actions
code: # Minimal planning problem pu <- data.frame(   id = 1:4,   cost = c(2, 3, 1, 4) )  features <- data.frame(   id = c("sp1", "sp2") )  dist_features <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4),   feature = c("sp1", "sp2", "sp1", "sp2", "sp1", "sp2"),   amount = c(1, 2, 1, 3, 2, 1) )  p <- inputData(   pu = pu,   features = features,   dist_features = dist_features ) #> Error: features$id must be numeric/integer ids (got non-numeric strings).  actions <- data.frame(   id = c("conservation", "restoration"),   name = c("Conservation", "Restoration") )  # Example 1: all actions feasible everywhere, with action-level costs p1 <- add_actions(   x = p,   actions = actions,   cost = c(conservation = 5, restoration = 12) ) #> Error: object 'p' not found  head(p1$data$dist_actions) #> Error: object 'p1' not found  # Example 2: specify feasibility explicitly include_df <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 2, 3, 4),   action = c("conservation", "conservation", "restoration", "restoration") )  p2 <- add_actions(   x = p,   actions = actions,   include = include_df,   cost = 10 ) #> Error: object 'p' not found  p2$data$dist_actions #> Error: object 'p2' not found  # Example 3: exclude selected pairs after full expansion exclude_df <- data.frame(   pu = c(2, 4),   action = c("restoration", "conservation") )  p3 <- add_actions(   x = p,   actions = actions,   exclude = exclude_df,   cost = c(conservation = 3, restoration = 8) ) #> Error: object 'p' not found  p3$data$dist_actions #> Error: object 'p3' not found

title: Add action effects to a planning problem — add_effects
text: function provides unified interface specifying action effects several input formats enforcing single internal representation. Regardless user supplies effects, stored output always follows canonical structure based non-negative benefit/loss components. Let \(b_{}\) denote baseline amount feature \(f\) planning unit \(\), taken x$data$dist_features$amount. Let \(\Delta_{f}\) denote net change caused applying action \(\) planning unit \(\) feature \(f\). canonical stored representation : $$ \mathrm{benefit}_{f} = \max(\Delta_{f}, 0), $$ $$ \mathrm{loss}_{f} = \max(-\Delta_{f}, 0). $$ Hence: \(\Delta_{f} > 0\), benefit > 0 loss = 0, \(\Delta_{f} < 0\), benefit = 0 loss > 0, \(\Delta_{f} = 0\), zero. split effects benefit loss? representation avoids ambiguity downstream optimization models. allows package support, example, objectives maximize beneficial effects, minimize damages, impose -net-loss conditions, combine components differently multi-objective formulations. Supported effect specifications effects argument may provided one following forms: NULL. empty effects table stored. data.frame(action, feature, multiplier). case, effects constructed multiplying baseline feature amounts supplied multiplier: $$   \Delta_{f} = b_{} \times m_{f},   $$ \(m_{f}\) multiplier associated action \(\) feature \(f\). specification expanded feasible (pu, action) pairs. data.frame(pu, action, feature, ...) giving explicit effects individual triples. table may contain: delta effect: interpreted signed net changes, : interpreted -action amounts effect_type = "", benefit /loss: explicit non-negative split components, legacy signed benefit without loss: interpreted signed net effect backwards compatibility. named list terra::SpatRaster objects, one per action. case, names must match action ids, raster must contain one layer per feature. Raster values aggregated planning-unit level using effect_aggregation. Interpretation effect_type effect_type = "delta", supplied values interpreted net changes directly. effect_type = "", supplied values interpreted -action amounts converted internally net effects using: $$ \Delta_{f} = \mathrm{}_{f} - b_{}. $$ Missing baseline values treated zero. Feasibility locked-decisions Effects retained feasible (pu, action) pairs listed x$data$dist_actions. Thus, add_actions() must called first. drop_locked_out = TRUE x$data$dist_actions$status exists, rows associated status == 3 removed storing final effects table. Duplicate rows semantic validation multiple rows supplied (pu, action, feature) triple, aggregated summing benefit loss separately. resulting triple must still respect package semantics, namely components strictly positive simultaneously. Inputs violating rule rejected. Filtering canonicalization validation, rows can filtered using filter = "", "benefit", "loss". default, zero-effect rows removed unless keep_zero = TRUE. Stored output resulting table x$data$dist_effects contains user-facing ids, internal integer ids, optional labels actions features. Metadata describing stored representation input interpretation written x$data$effects_meta.

title: Add action effects to a planning problem — add_effects
code: # Minimal problem pu <- data.frame(   id = 1:3,   cost = c(1, 2, 3) )  features <- data.frame(   id = 1:2,   name = c("sp1", "sp2") )  dist_features <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 1, 2, 3),   feature = c(1, 2, 1, 2),   amount = c(10, 5, 8, 4) )  p <- inputData(   pu = pu,   features = features,   dist_features = dist_features )  actions <- data.frame(   id = c("conservation", "restoration") )  p <- add_actions(   x = p,   actions = actions,   cost = c(conservation = 2, restoration = 4) )  # 1) Empty effects p0 <- add_effects(p, effects = NULL) #> Warning: All effect values are zero after filtering. p0$data$dist_effects #>  [1] pu               action           feature          benefit          #>  [5] loss             internal_pu      internal_action  internal_feature #>  [9] feature_name     action_name      #> <0 rows> (or 0-length row.names)  # 2) Multipliers by action and feature mult <- data.frame(   action = c("conservation", "restoration"),   feature = c("sp1", "sp2"),   multiplier = c(0.10, -0.25) )  p1 <- add_effects(   x = p,   effects = mult,   effect_type = "delta" )  p1$data$dist_effects #>   pu       action feature benefit loss internal_pu internal_action #> 1  1 conservation       1     1.0 0.00           1               1 #> 2  2 conservation       1     0.8 0.00           2               1 #> 7  1  restoration       2     0.0 1.25           1               2 #> 8  3  restoration       2     0.0 1.00           3               2 #>   internal_feature feature_name  action_name #> 1                1          sp1 conservation #> 2                1          sp1 conservation #> 7                2          sp2  restoration #> 8                2          sp2  restoration  # 3) Explicit net effects eff <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 2, 3),   action = c("conservation", "restoration", "restoration"),   feature = c(1, 1, 2),   delta = c(2, -3, 1) )  p2 <- add_effects(p, effects = eff) p2$data$dist_effects #>   pu       action feature benefit loss internal_pu internal_action #> 1  1 conservation       1       2    0           1               1 #> 2  2  restoration       1       0    3           2               2 #> 3  3  restoration       2       1    0           3               2 #>   internal_feature feature_name  action_name #> 1                1          sp1 conservation #> 2                1          sp1  restoration #> 3                2          sp2  restoration  # 4) Keep only beneficial effects p3 <- add_effects(p, effects = eff, filter = "benefit") p3$data$dist_effects #>   pu       action feature benefit loss internal_pu internal_action #> 1  1 conservation       1       2    0           1               1 #> 3  3  restoration       2       1    0           3               2 #>   internal_feature feature_name  action_name #> 1                1          sp1 conservation #> 3                2          sp2  restoration

title: Create a planning problem input object — inputData
text: Overview role inputData() construct internal input data model used mosap. Regardless input mode, function aims produce consistent problem representation containing, minimum: planning-unit table x$data$pu, feature table x$data$features, feature-distribution table x$data$dist_features. Internally, means problem always reduced tabular core form: planning units \(\\mathcal{P}\), features \(f \\mathcal{F}\), amounts \(a_{} \ge 0\) stored dist_features, , available, spatial metadata associated planning units. Thus, inputData() run, landscape represented set planning units baseline feature amounts: $$ a_{} = \text{amount feature } f \text{ planning unit } . $$ baseline amounts later combined actions, effects, targets, objectives, constraints build optimization model. Supported input modes Four workflows supported. 1. Tabular mode pu, features, dist_features data.frame objects, problem built directly tabular data. spatial package required. mode appropriate user already tabular planning-unit table, feature catalog, planning unit–feature distribution table. 2. Vector-PU spatial mode dist_features missing NULL, pu spatial vector object, features raster stack, function derives dist_features aggregating raster values planning-unit polygons. mode: polygon becomes one planning unit, raster layer becomes one feature, feature amounts aggregated polygon, currently using sum, planning-unit cost taken either vector attribute column cost raster aggregated polygons using cost_aggregation. 3. Raster-cell fast mode dist_features missing NULL, pu features rasters, function enters raster-cell workflow valid raster cell becomes one planning unit. mode: pu used mask template, cost must single-layer raster, raster cell becomes planning unit : $$   \text{\code{pu} NA} \quad \text{} \quad   \text{\code{cost} finite } > 0,   $$ valid cell assigned new sequential planning-unit id, feature values extracted directly cell without raster--polygon conversion, feature values equal NA treated zero building dist_features, strictly positive feature amounts stored x$data$dist_features. mode substantially faster converting large rasters polygons generally preferred option large regular grids. 4. Hybrid sf-tabular mode pu sf object, features dist_features data.frames, problem built supplied tabular feature distribution preserving planning-unit geometry raw spatial attributes. mode useful user already curated tabular dist_features table still wants retain geometry later spatial operations boundary construction, plotting, spatial feasibility specifications. stored resulting object modes, returned object contains tabular planning core. Depending input mode, additional components may also stored: x$data$pu Planning-unit table, always present. x$data$features Feature table, always present. x$data$dist_features Baseline planning unit–feature amounts, always present. x$data$pu_data_raw Raw planning-unit attributes aligned planning-unit id, available. x$data$pu_coords Planning-unit coordinates, available derivable. x$data$pu_sf Planning-unit geometry sf, available safely alignable. x$data$pu_raster_id Raster mask/template used derive planning units raster cells rasterized planning units, available. x$data$cell_index Indices valid raster cells raster-cell mode. x$data$cost_raster Stored cost raster raster-cell mode. x$data$features_raster Stored feature raster stack raster-cell mode. Spatial relations created automatically function create boundary, adjacency, distance, spatial relations automatically. Spatial relations must added explicitly afterwards using functions : add_spatial_boundary, add_spatial_rook, add_spatial_queen, add_spatial_knn, add_spatial_distance, add_spatial_relations. raster-cell mode, spatial relations typically created coordinates rather polygons, example using add_spatial_knn add_spatial_distance. Locks interpreted automatically function interpret planning-unit lock information, even input data contain fields locked_in, locked_out, Marxan-style status codes. information may preserved x$data$pu_data_raw, activated automatically. Locked planning units defined later add_locked_pu. Feature identifiers Features internally standardized id-based representation. spatial modes raster layers used, one feature created per raster layer, : id = 1, 2, ..., nlyr(features), name equal raster layer name, available, otherwise generated name form "feature.1", "feature.2", . Planning-unit identifiers vector hybrid modes, planning-unit ids taken column named pu_id_col. column missing pu_id_col = "id", sequential ids created warning. raster-cell mode, planning-unit ids always created sequentially valid raster cells retained masking cost filtering. Cost handling purely tabular mode, cost used generic method planning-unit costs expected already present pu table supplied internal tabular builder. spatial modes, cost required: vector-PU mode, may either: name numeric attribute column planning-unit layer, cost raster aggregated polygons; raster-cell mode, must single-layer raster aligned pu features; hybrid sf + tabular mode, must either: name attribute column sf object, omitted sf attributes already contain column literally named cost. Ordering alignment spatial modes, planning units, derived coordinates, raw attributes, geometry, extracted feature amounts aligned planning-unit id order final Problem object created. alignment critical later functions assume stored planning-unit components refer ordered set planning units.

title: Create a planning problem input object — inputData
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # ------------------------------------------------------ # 1) Tabular mode # ------------------------------------------------------ pu <- data.frame(id = 1:3, cost = c(1, 2, 3)) features <- data.frame(id = 1:2, name = c("sp1", "sp2")) dist_features <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 1, 2, 3),   feature = c(1, 2, 2, 1),   amount = c(1, 5, 2, 1) )  x <- inputData(   pu = pu,   features = features,   dist_features = dist_features )  # ------------------------------------------------------ # 2) Raster-cell fast mode # ------------------------------------------------------ library(terra) pu_mask <- rast("pu_mask.tif") cost_r  <- rast("cost.tif") feat_r  <- rast(c("sp1.tif", "sp2.tif"))  x <- inputData(   pu = pu_mask,   features = feat_r,   cost = cost_r )  # ------------------------------------------------------ # 3) Vector-PU spatial mode # ------------------------------------------------------ pu_v   <- vect("pus.gpkg") feat_r <- rast(c("sp1.tif", "sp2.tif")) cost_r <- rast("cost.tif")  x <- inputData(   pu = pu_v,   features = feat_r,   cost = cost_r,   pu_id_col = "id",   cost_aggregation = "mean" )  # ------------------------------------------------------ # 4) Hybrid sf + tabular mode # ------------------------------------------------------ library(sf) pu_sf <- st_read("pus.gpkg") features <- data.frame(id = 1:2, name = c("sp1", "sp2")) dist_features <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 1, 2, 3),   feature = c("sp1", "sp2", "sp2", "sp1"),   amount = c(1, 5, 2, 1) )  x <- inputData(   pu = pu_sf,   features = features,   dist_features = dist_features,   cost = "cost" ) } # }

title: Set the AUGMECON multi-objective method — set_method_augmecon
text: General idea Suppose \(m \ge 2\) objective functions registered problem: $$ f_1(x), f_2(x), \dots, f_m(x). $$ AUGMECON selects one primary objective, say \(f_p(x)\), treats remaining \(m - 1\) objectives secondary objectives. fixed combination \(\epsilon\)-levels, method solves sequence single-objective subproblems form: $$ \max \; f_p(x) + \rho \sum_{k \\mathcal{S}} \frac{s_k}{R_k} $$ subject $$ f_k(x) - s_k = \epsilon_k, \qquad k \\mathcal{S}, $$ $$ s_k \ge 0, \qquad k \\mathcal{S}, $$ together original feasibility constraints planning problem. : \(f_p(x)\) primary objective, \(\mathcal{S}\) set secondary objectives, \(\epsilon_k\) imposed level secondary objective \(k\), \(s_k\) non-negative slack variable, \(R_k\) payoff-table range used normalize objective \(k\), \(\rho > 0\) small augmentation coefficient. original AUGMECON formulation Mavrotas (2009), augmentation term ensures , among solutions primary objective value, solver prefers larger slack, thereby avoiding weakly efficient points improving Pareto-front generation. Secondary-objective equalities slacks key difference standard \(\epsilon\)-constraint AUGMECON secondary objectives written equalities slacks rather simple inequalities. maximization-type secondary objective, takes form: $$ f_k(x) - s_k = \epsilon_k, \qquad s_k \ge 0. $$ implies: $$ f_k(x) \ge \epsilon_k, $$ explicitly measuring excess imposed \(\epsilon\)-level \(s_k\). augmentation term rewards excess normalized form. implementation terms, exact sign convention objective depends whether internally treated minimization maximization objective, method always preserves AUGMECON principle: one objective optimized directly, others turned constrained objectives, non-negative slacks measure controlled deviation imposed \(\epsilon\)-levels, primary objective augmented small slack-based reward. Automatic manual grids AUGMECON requires grid \(\epsilon\)-levels secondary objective. grid supplied, must named list one numeric vector per secondary objective. vector defines exact \(\epsilon\)-levels explored objective. grid = NULL, grid generated automatically later solve. case, method first computes extreme points payoff-table ranges secondary objectives, generates n_points levels one. include_extremes = TRUE, automatic grid includes extreme values secondary objective. lexicographic = TRUE, extreme points computed using lexicographic anchoring, can improve numerical stability payoff table quality objectives tightly competing. tolerance used lexicographic anchoring controlled lexicographic_tol. Normalization augmentation augmentation term scaled using payoff-table ranges secondary objectives. \(R_k\) denotes range secondary objective \(k\), effective coefficient applied slack : $$ \frac{\rho}{R_k}, $$ \(\rho = \code{augmentation}\). normalization important different objectives may measured different numerical scales. Without normalization, slack belonging large-scale objective dominate augmentation term simply due units. implementation, user supplies augmentation base coefficient \(\rho\), normalized slack coefficients computed internally solve time using corresponding payoff-table ranges. code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # ------------------------------------------------------------ # Example 1: cost vs fragmentation # ------------------------------------------------------------ pu <- data.frame(id = 1:4, cost = c(1, 2, 2, 3)) features <- data.frame(id = 1, name = "sp1") dist_features <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 2, 3, 4),   feature = 1,   amount = c(1, 1, 1, 1) )  x <- inputData(   pu = pu,   features = features,   dist_features = dist_features )  bnd <- data.frame(   id1 = c(1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 4),   id2 = c(1, 2, 3, 2, 3, 4, 4),   boundary = c(2, 2, 2, 1, 1, 1, 2) )  x <- add_spatial_boundary(x, boundary = bnd, name = "boundary")  x <- x |>   add_targets_relative(0.5) |>   add_objective_min_cost(alias = "cost") |>   add_objective_min_fragmentation(     alias = "frag",     relation_name = "boundary",     weight_multiplier = 0.01   )  x <- set_method_weighted(   x,   aliases = c("cost", "frag"),   weights = c(1, 1),   normalize_weights = TRUE )  # sol <- solve(x)  # ------------------------------------------------------------ # Example 2: scan weights to explore trade-offs # ------------------------------------------------------------ weight_grid <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.25) xs <- vector("list", length(weight_grid))  for (i in seq_along(weight_grid)) {   w <- weight_grid[i]   xs[[i]] <- set_method_weighted(     x,     aliases = c("cost", "frag"),     weights = c(1 - w, w),     normalize_weights = TRUE   )   # sols[[i]] <- solve(xs[[i]]) }  # ------------------------------------------------------------ # Example 3: request objective scaling # ------------------------------------------------------------ x <- set_method_weighted(   x,   aliases = c("cost", "frag"),   weights = c(0.7, 0.3),   normalize_weights = FALSE,   objective_scaling = TRUE ) } # }

title: mosap 1.0.4
text: First CRAN release mosap. Provides modular workflow exact multi-objective spatial planning based mixed-integer programming (MIP). Introduces core Problem, Solution, SolutionSet classes. Adds support modular problem construction inputData(), add_*(), set_*(), solve(). Supports atomic objective registration multi-objective solution methods, including weighted-sum, epsilon-constraint, AUGMECON. Includes support spatial relations boundary, rook, queen, k-nearest neighbours, distance-based relations. Supports commercial open-source solvers, including Gurobi, CPLEX, CBC, SYMPHONY. Adds user-facing extraction visualization tools planning units, actions, features, targets, spatial outputs, trade-offs. Includes substantial updates documentation, package structure, contribution guidelines.
